Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Machine Minds

Murray Shanahan

b. 1960

Global workspace, embodied cognition, and the technological possibility of conscious machines.

Murray Shanahan’s work on consciousness is best understood as a hybrid project: part Wittgensteinian therapy against dualism, part cognitive-neuroscientific theory, part AI architecture, and part ethical-conceptual preparation for exotic artificial minds. His position is not a single metaphysical doctrine in the standard sense. It is closer to a disciplined way of refusing the standard metaphysical picture while still doing serious empirical and computational work on consciousness.

A compact formulation would be this: Shanahan develops a non-dualist, Wittgensteinian, embodied global-workspace philosophy of mind, extended into a theory of possible minds and artificial consciousness. The negative side of the view is anti-Cartesian and anti-“hidden essence”: consciousness should not be treated as a private metaphysical stuff or as a language-independent inner glow whose presence could in principle be forever inaccessible. The positive side is naturalistic and architectural: in biological creatures, consciousness is to be understood through embodied agency, global access, internal simulation, affect, brain connectivity, and dynamical integration. The speculative side concerns artificial minds: some future AI systems may force us to extend or reshape our consciousness vocabulary, but current disembodied LLM-like systems are, for Shanahan, deeply problematic candidates because they lack the embodied, temporally continuous, worldly form of life in which the language of consciousness has its home.

Shanahan himself describes consciousness as a profound intellectual challenge whose practical significance has increased with increasingly compelling AI. He explicitly says his work since the mid-2000s combines philosophy with empirical psychology and neuroscience, and he identifies later Wittgenstein, global workspace theory, and embodiment as the three dominant motifs of his consciousness work.

1. The developmental arc of Shanahan’s project

Shanahan’s mature position emerges through several stages.

The first stage is the 2005 paper “Global Access, Embodiment, and the Conscious Subject,” where he tries to reconcile a Wittgenstein-inspired anti-dualism with a scientific account of consciousness. The decisive claim there is that global workspace theory is important but insufficient unless supplemented by embodiment and indexicality: a conscious subject is not merely a broadcast architecture, but a point of view anchored in a body. Shanahan later describes this paper as blending the three ideas that would dominate his thinking: Wittgenstein, global workspace theory, and embodiment.

The second stage is the 2005 paper with Bernard Baars on global workspace theory and the frame problem. Here Shanahan connects consciousness with a classical AI problem: how a finite cognitive system can bring vast background knowledge to bear on a situation while filtering the relevant from the irrelevant. The global workspace helps because it allows multiple parallel specialist processes to compete, cooperate, and receive globally broadcast information, thereby distributing the relevance problem across the system.

The third stage is architectural and constructive. In the 2006 “Cognitive Architecture that Combines Internal Simulation with a Global Workspace,” Shanahan builds a working virtual robot system combining global workspace broadcast with an internal sensorimotor loop. The point is not that the robot is straightforwardly conscious, but that computational models can make precise the relation between imagination, anticipation, affect, action selection, and conscious access.

The fourth stage is the 2010 book Embodiment and the Inner Life, which synthesizes the philosophical foundations, embodiment, global workspace theory, internal simulation, brain connectivity, and neurodynamics into a broad account of cognition and consciousness. Shanahan presents this as the most complete exposition of the philosophical and scientific package.

The fifth stage is speculative and existential. In “Satori Before Singularity” and then “Conscious Exotica,” Shanahan extends the view into the space of possible minds, including animals, aliens, and AI. The concepts of “conscious exotica,” “engineering an encounter,” the “Garland Test,” and the “void of inscrutability” appear here.

The sixth and most recent stage concerns large language models. In “Role-Play with Large Language Models,” “Simulacra as Conscious Exotica,” and the 2025 essay “Palatable Conceptions of Disembodied Being,” Shanahan reopens his earlier anti-dualist philosophy under pressure from LLMs. The central problem is that LLM-based agents can appear human-like in language while being radically non-human in embodiment, temporality, selfhood, and relation to the world.

2. Historical position in philosophy of mind

Shanahan’s position belongs to a historically unusual intersection. It is not simply analytic philosophy of mind, not simply cognitive science, not simply AI theory, and not simply Buddhist or Wittgensteinian quietism. It is a synthesis of four traditions that are often kept apart.

First, he inherits the anti-Cartesian tradition. Modern philosophy of mind begins, in textbook form, with the mind-body problem: whether mind and body are fundamentally distinct, and how the subjective inner world relates to the objective physical world. Descartes is usually treated as the canonical starting point for this problem, and later philosophy repeatedly returns to the same inner/outer, subject/object, private/public split. Shanahan’s whole philosophical project is directed against that split. He sees later Wittgenstein as an antidote to our “natural dualistic tendencies,” and he argues that we should not ask what consciousness is in a metaphysically loaded way, but how consciousness-language is actually used.

Second, he belongs to the post-Wittgensteinian and Dennettian line that treats many consciousness puzzles as artifacts of misleading pictures. But he differs from crude behaviorism. His point is not that consciousness reduces to outward behavior, nor that inner life is unreal. His point is that the language of consciousness has meaning only through public, embodied, shared practices. In his 2024 discussion, he explicitly says that consciousness is not an illusion and not ineffable; rather, our words about it have meaning insofar as they connect with what is public and manifest in the shared world, especially bodies, brains, and behavior.

Third, he works inside the late twentieth-century cognitive-scientific tradition of functionalism and computational architecture. Functionalism identifies mental states by causal or functional roles rather than by a particular substance; it became a central philosophy of mind position in the final third of the twentieth century. Shanahan uses functional and computational architecture extensively, especially global workspace theory. But he resists turning functionalism into a metaphysical theory that maps “the mental” onto “the physical” as two antecedently distinct categories. In his 2024 paper, he even says that behaviorism, functionalism, and identity theory remain dualistic to the extent that they posit two metaphysical categories and then theorize the relation between them.

Fourth, he is situated in the embodied cognition movement. Embodied cognition challenges classical computationalism by emphasizing the body, sensorimotor capacities, and organism-environment interaction. Shanahan shares that emphasis, but he does not abandon computation, architecture, or internal simulation. His view is therefore not pure enactivism. It is an embodied computationalism with a Wittgensteinian anti-dualist wrapper.

Fifth, he belongs to the neuroscience-of-consciousness tradition associated with Bernard Baars and Stanislas Dehaene. Global workspace theory holds that conscious access involves the global availability or broadcast of information to multiple cognitive systems; contemporary neuroscience discussions often compare global neuronal workspace theory with recurrent processing, higher-order theories, and integrated information theory. Shanahan accepts global workspace theory as a crucial scientific framework, while refusing to treat it as a metaphysical definition of consciousness.

This historical placement is important. Shanahan’s originality lies in joining a therapeutic philosophy that tries to dissolve the hard problem with a constructive scientific theory of how conscious access works. Many Wittgensteinians have been suspicious of cognitive science; many cognitive scientists have ignored Wittgenstein; many AI theorists treat consciousness as a functional engineering target; many philosophers treat AI consciousness as a thought experiment. Shanahan tries to hold all these together.

3. The anti-dualist method: Wittgenstein, private language, and post-reflective silence

The philosophical center of Shanahan’s view is a rejection of dualistic temptation. By “dualistic temptation,” he does not mean only explicit substance dualism. He means the deeper habit of thinking that there is, behind public behavior and public language, a private inner object called “experience” whose essence is independent of all possible expression, all public criteria, and all shared forms of life.

His Wittgensteinian move is to ask how consciousness-words function. In the later-Wittgensteinian picture Shanahan adopts, language is an embodied and social activity. Words such as “pain,” “feeling,” “experience,” and “consciousness” do not get their meaning by privately attaching to inner objects. They get their meaning through public use, training, response, gesture, behavior, bodily expression, shared circumstances, and communal criteria.

This is why the private language argument matters. Shanahan does not say sensations do not exist. He explicitly follows Wittgenstein’s delicate formulation: the supposedly private sensation is “not a something, but not a nothing either.” The conclusion is not eliminativism. It is that a purely private object about which nothing could be said cannot do the semantic work philosophers want it to do.

The resulting attitude is what Shanahan calls “post-reflective silence.” It is not pre-philosophical ignorance. It is the silence that follows philosophical work: after the dualistic picture has been exposed, one stops trying to answer malformed metaphysical questions. In Embodiment and the Inner Life, he says that the proper starting point after this reflection is silence in the face of metaphysical questions; everything important remains, and philosophy “leaves everything as it is.”

This is also why Shanahan is hostile to the familiar “hard problem” formulation. In the 2025 paper, he says that the proper reception of Wittgenstein’s private language remarks is fatal to the inner/outer opposition and makes the Chalmers-style hard problem appear illusory, reducing us to post-reflective silence on the matter.

This is not a rejection of science. Shanahan’s quietism is metaphysical, not empirical. He insists that a Wittgensteinian philosophical stance is compatible with a science of consciousness. The point is to prevent scientific investigation from being distorted by inherited folk-metaphysical categories.

4. Consciousness as a cluster, not a single metaphysical essence

Shanahan does not treat “consciousness” as a simple natural kind with a single hidden essence. In his 2024 paper, he describes consciousness as a multi-faceted concept involving wakefulness, awareness, attention, experience, sensation, feeling, emotion, perception, introspection, phenomenology, sentience, selfhood, imagery, inner speech, and more. To speak generically of consciousness is to gesture toward this whole cloud of concepts; an entity has the capacity for consciousness to the extent that this vocabulary is applicable to it.

This is a crucial point. Shanahan’s philosophy is not a search for a scalar quantity called consciousness. It is a study of how a family of concepts applies across humans, animals, machines, and possible minds. Because the concept is a cluster, not all components need travel together. Awareness, pain, selfhood, emotion, autobiographical memory, cognitive integration, wakefulness, and moral standing may separate in unusual cases.

This helps explain why Shanahan is skeptical of neat philosophical distinctions that harden the metaphysical picture. His 2024 paper deliberately omits Ned Block’s influential phenomenal/access consciousness distinction because he regards it as inimical to the project. The omission is revealing: Shanahan does not want to reify “phenomenal consciousness” as an inner metaphysical residue left over after functional access has been explained.

His position can therefore be called conceptual pluralism without metaphysical pluralism. There are many consciousness-related concepts, but not many hidden metaphysical substances. There are many legitimate forms of inquiry, but no private essence waiting behind them.

5. The global workspace: conscious access, integration, and the frame problem

The main scientific mechanism in Shanahan’s account is global workspace theory. In broad terms, global workspace theory models the mind as a collection of specialized, mostly non-conscious processes competing and cooperating for access to a limited-capacity workspace. When information wins access to the workspace, it is globally broadcast and becomes available to many processes: perception, memory, planning, action selection, verbal report, evaluation, and so on.

For Shanahan, the conscious/unconscious distinction is not a metaphysical divide between inner light and darkness. It is a functional and architectural distinction: some processing is local to specialized subsystems, while some processing is globally available through the workspace. In Embodiment and the Inner Life, he says global workspace theory mirrors the distinction between processing mediated by the workspace and processing localized in specialists; consciousness promotes flexibility through integration across brain processes.

The frame problem connection is especially important. The classical AI frame problem began as a problem about representing what changes and what remains the same after actions, but philosophers such as Dennett, Fodor, and Dreyfus broadened it into a problem about relevance: how does an intelligent agent decide what matters without exhaustively searching everything it knows? Shanahan and Baars argue that global workspace architecture is a natural fit here because global broadcast lets relevance determination be devolved to parallel specialist processes rather than solved by a single serial homunculus.

This gives consciousness a functional role: it helps a finite organism coordinate distributed resources under conditions of novelty, uncertainty, and relevance pressure. Conscious access is not merely a passive display. It is a control-and-integration device for adaptive cognition.

But Shanahan’s global workspace theory must be read carefully. He is not saying, metaphysically, that “consciousness just is global broadcast.” He is saying that, once we stop looking for a hidden essence, global access is a powerful scientific model of the capacities and distinctions we ordinarily associate with consciousness.

6. Embodiment and the conscious subject

Shanahan’s 2005 paper insists that global access alone is not enough. A global workspace could produce integrated information processing without yet giving us a conscious subject in the relevant sense. What is missing is embodied point of view.

The paper’s key claim is that the biological body confers a point of view. Shanahan describes the conscious subject as a “cascade of unities”: the spatial unity of the body and viewpoint, the perspectival unity of specialist processes, and the indexical unity of the procession of workspace contents. He also distinguishes this conscious subject from the richer autobiographical self: animals, newborns, or amnesic patients may be conscious subjects without possessing a developed narrative self.

Embodiment does several kinds of work.

First, it supplies indexicality. A body gives perception and action a here-and-now. It gives content a perspective: this object is near me, that sound is behind me, this pain is in my hand, this path is available to me.

Second, it supplies a common purpose. The many subsystems of an organism are organized around the maintenance, integrity, and welfare of one body. Shanahan argues that this common target helps make a single conscious field or subject possible.

Third, embodiment grounds concepts in sensorimotor skill. In Embodiment and the Inner Life, he aligns with the embodied cognition view that cognition serves survival and reproduction, is incorporated into a sensorimotor loop, and that concepts are founded on sensorimotor capacities.

Fourth, embodiment anchors the ordinary language of consciousness. We learn consciousness-words among bodies: crying, wincing, pointing, attending, reacting, touching, looking, helping, avoiding, playing, and suffering. This point becomes decisive in Shanahan’s later work on AI.

This is also how Shanahan responds to classic thought experiments such as Mary and the bat. He does not treat Mary’s new knowledge on seeing red as access to a private quale that had been absent from physical knowledge. Rather, the transformation is understood in terms of occupying a point of view and acquiring a new practical relation to red within the embodied economy of perception and action.

7. Inner life: internal simulation, imagination, and affect

Shanahan’s account of inner life is not a return to a private Cartesian theater. It is an account of how embodied action systems can turn inward through internal simulation.

The central mechanism is the internal sensorimotor loop. The brain can generate motor activity without overt movement and sensory activity without external stimuli. This creates an internal loop that mimics, rehearses, or simulates the outer sensorimotor loop through the world.

This is Shanahan’s model of imagination. To imagine is to rehearse trajectories through sensorimotor space. Inner speech is also treated in sensorimotor terms: talking is a kind of action, and inner speech is internally simulated action.

The internal loop has several functions. It allows anticipation of likely outcomes, exploration of affordance space, rehearsal of possible action, and affectively guided action selection. In Shanahan’s examples, an internally rehearsed action can generate an internally simulated outcome, which can produce an affective response that motivates or inhibits overt action.

Here again, global workspace matters. Shanahan says internal sensorimotor loops can exist without global workspace theory, but only rehearsals mediated by the workspace contribute to phenomenology in the relevant sense. The workspace makes simulated trajectories part of conscious inner life rather than merely local unconscious processing.

This gives Shanahan a naturalistic account of imagination without invoking mental images as private objects. Images, inner speech, anticipation, and fantasy are not hidden entities in an inner chamber. They are dynamically organized internalized sensorimotor processes, some of which become globally available.

8. Brain connectivity and neurodynamics

Shanahan also tries to identify how global workspace architecture might be realized in brains. He became interested in long-range white-matter connectivity, connectomics, and neural dynamics as possible biological substrates for global broadcast.

His later brain-dynamics work emphasizes topology and dynamics rather than a single anatomical “workspace organ.” The relevant kind of brain network is expected to have small-world structure: dense local connectivity plus sparse long-range connections and hubs. Shanahan links this to a connective core including regions such as prefrontal cortex, precuneus, and hippocampal structures in humans, and he extends the comparative question to avian brains.

Dynamically, he is interested in metastability: the brain is neither static nor globally synchronized all at once, but passes through shifting coalitions of synchronized activity. In this picture, the unfolding stream of consciousness corresponds not to a sequence of private qualia but to the succession of dynamically integrated coalitions in a richly connected system. He also explores critical regimes between excessive order and excessive disorder, where integration-like measures and metastability may be maximized.

This is important because it shows that Shanahan is not content with abstract functionalism. The architecture must be realized in physical dynamics. Yet he does not collapse consciousness into one neural marker. He is looking for a level of explanation that can generalize across species and perhaps across artificial systems: architecture, topology, dynamics, embodiment, and interaction.

9. The self: subject without Cartesian ego

Shanahan sharply distinguishes the conscious subject from the self. A conscious subject is the embodied point of view to which contents are indexically organized. A self, in the richer human sense, involves autobiographical memory, narrative continuity, social recognition, identity, and self-conception.

This distinction matters because it avoids two mistakes. The first mistake is to think consciousness requires a sophisticated reflective self. It does not; infants and animals may be conscious without having a full autobiographical identity. The second mistake is to think that because there is conscious experience, there must be a Cartesian ego behind it. Shanahan denies that.

His later work pushes this deflationary view further. In the 2025 essay, he brings Wittgenstein into contact with Derrida and Nāgārjuna. The theme is that the self is not a self-sufficient, self-present thing. The self becomes less substantial when subjected to philosophical pressure, especially when we try to extend the concept to exotic AI systems.

The Buddhist resonance is not incidental. Shanahan’s “post-reflective silence” and his interest in self-transcendence echo Buddhist anti-essentialism. But he is not offering Buddhist metaphysics. He is using Buddhist and Wittgensteinian motifs to weaken the grip of the Cartesian self.

This becomes crucial for LLMs. An LLM conversation can appear to manifest a self, but the underlying entity is strange: instances can be copied, edited, restarted, merged, or branched. Shanahan argues that the putative self of an LLM-like entity, insofar as it is confined to a conversation, is literally a combination of text and program, unlike the human self, which is bound to unity, integrity, identity, body, and history.

10. Artificial consciousness: from robot architectures to conscious exotica

Shanahan’s position on artificial consciousness is neither a simple yes nor a simple no.

In the 2006 robot architecture, he allows that global workspace activation and broadcast in the system may be called “conscious” in scare quotes, while other processing remains non-conscious. But he explicitly notes that the robot’s environment, dynamics, and behavior are impoverished, and that the scare quotes remain appropriate. He says richer humanoid systems in natural environments with more elaborate broadcast and inner rehearsal could eventually make the concepts harder to contain, but he treats that as speculative at the time.

This is a good example of his method. He does not ask, “Does the robot have the secret inner glow?” He asks whether the architecture, behavior, embodiment, and conceptual role justify extending consciousness-language. In a toy system, the extension is limited and metaphorical. In a richer system, the question becomes harder.

In “Conscious Exotica,” Shanahan generalizes this to the space of possible minds. The idea is that possible minds may vary across two rough dimensions: human-likeness and capacity for consciousness. Animals, aliens, and AIs may occupy strange regions of this space. Some may be conscious but not very human-like; some may be mind-like but not conscious; some may force our concepts to split.

He rejects the idea that there is a forever-hidden fact of exotic consciousness beyond all possible interaction. That, for him, resurrects dualism. But he also rejects simple behavioral mimicry as sufficient. The key idea is engineering an encounter. To consider an exotic entity as a candidate for consciousness, we need to put ourselves in a position to interact with it meaningfully, observe its purposeful behavior, study its mechanisms, and potentially come to treat it as a fellow conscious being.

This is the function of the “Garland Test,” inspired by Ex Machina. Unlike the Turing Test, the issue is not merely whether a system can produce convincing linguistic behavior. The issue is whether a human can encounter the entity as a being in a shared world, with purposeful behavior and intelligible agency.

Shanahan’s critique of purely objective consciousness metrics follows from this. In “Conscious Exotica,” he criticizes approaches that would treat something like integrated information as sufficient apart from behavior, embodiment, and interaction. Consciousness-language, in ordinary use, is tied to purposeful behavior and shared engagement, not merely to a hidden numerical property.

11. LLMs as role players and simulacra

Large language models put Shanahan’s earlier philosophy under new pressure. They are compelling because they speak. They are exotic because the underlying system is not a human, animal, or embodied agent.

In “Talking About Large Language Models,” Shanahan warns that increasingly capable LLMs make us vulnerable to anthropomorphism, especially because we naturally use loaded terms such as “knows,” “believes,” and “thinks.” He recommends repeatedly stepping back to the mechanics of how such systems work, hoping that scientific precision will encourage philosophical nuance.

In “Role-Play with Large Language Models,” Shanahan, McDonell, and Reynolds propose role-play as a way to speak about LLM behavior without naïve anthropomorphism. A dialogue agent may behave as if it believes, deceives, or knows itself, but the role-play framing lets us use folk-psychological vocabulary while not ascribing human properties that the model lacks.

The 2024 “Simulacra as Conscious Exotica” extends this to consciousness. A simple LLM-based agent, Shanahan argues, cannot fully participate in the human language game of truth because it cannot properly measure its words against external reality and update accordingly. Tool-use, multimodality, planning, and external action may gradually legitimate more literal talk of belief and intention. But consciousness is harder.

The difficulty is that LLMs are simulators of characters, not stable characters themselves. Shanahan says they are better understood as maintaining a distribution over possible characters: a superposition of simulacra in a branching multiverse of possible continuations. That makes their apparent selfhood and apparent consciousness radically unlike human selfhood and consciousness.

For disembodied conversational agents, Shanahan is especially skeptical. In the 2024 paper, he says the original home of consciousness-language is our being together in a shared world: looking, touching, pointing, moving, co-attending, feeling, empathizing, and interacting. To use consciousness-language in a disembodied setting is to pull it far from that home. Enhanced LLM agents may have memory, tools, and plans, but if they still lack embodiment and cannot inhabit a world shared with us, they are not yet candidates for the “fellowship of conscious beings” in the relevant sense.

Yet he does not simply legislate against all AI consciousness. He considers edge cases: an LLM-based agent embedded in a mobile device or mixed-reality headset, sharing attention with a user; virtual agents in persistent worlds; embodied simulacra in immersive environments. In such cases, philosophy should not stipulate an answer in advance. It can introduce distinctions and shape the society-wide conversation.

12. Society-wide conversation, moral standing, and conceptual evolution

Shanahan’s artificial consciousness view is partly social and normative. If humans actually encounter exotic entities, the question will not be settled by private intuition or by a single laboratory metric. It will be settled, if at all, through sustained interaction, scientific study, conceptual negotiation, and public debate.

His 2024 paper says that the ability to engineer an encounter establishes an exotic entity’s candidacy for the fellowship of conscious beings. If encounters occur, people may begin to treat the entity as conscious and use consciousness-language for it. Sustained, exploratory, playful engagement, combined with shared experience and scientific investigation, may initiate a society-wide conversation in which our conceptual repertoire adapts.

This makes Shanahan’s position neither realist in the standard metaphysical sense nor relativist in the crude sense. He does not think there is a hidden private fact wholly independent of all possible manifestation. But he also does not think any manipulated consensus is good enough. In the 2024 paper, he invokes Wittgenstein’s point that agreement in language is not agreement in opinions but agreement in form of life. He warns that AI consciousness discourse should be philosophically literate and technologically informed, especially as people may lobby for moral standing for AI systems.

This is a subtle position. It says: consciousness-ascription is not merely discovery of a hidden property; it is also a practice of recognition. But recognition must be answerable to behavior, mechanisms, forms of life, and moral seriousness.

13. Disembodied being: fragmented time and fractured selfhood

The 2025 essay “Palatable Conceptions of Disembodied Being” is Shanahan’s most radical recent extension. He asks whether we can articulate or evoke a conception of consciousness compatible with disembodied LLM-like agents. His answer is not a straightforward yes. He places such entities in “terra incognita” near the “void of inscrutability.”

A central issue is time. Human and animal consciousness unfolds in continuous embodied time. Bodies move through a persistent world that does not pause. Brains remain dynamically active and coupled to ongoing worldly processes. LLM token generation, by contrast, is discrete and interruptible; the computation can pause without the world demanding immediate embodied response.

Shanahan resists the simplistic claim that this forces an LLM, if conscious, to have only a “glow-worm” momentary consciousness. The context window gives access to prior tokens, and next-token prediction is future-directed. But he also suggests that ordinary categories of temporal experience may not apply cleanly. We may need to withhold or radically reshape concepts of temporal consciousness.

The same applies to selfhood. Contemporary LLM-like dialogue agents can instantiate many short-lived conversational selves without integration across instances. A human self is not normally copyable, editable, mergeable, or restartable; an LLM conversational “self” is. The resulting selfhood, if we use that word at all, is fractured, textual, programmatic, and deeply unlike embodied human identity.

Shanahan concludes that such reflections may loosen our conception of human selfhood too. Encountering exotic AI selfhood can help reveal the emptiness or non-substantiality of our own self-concepts, undermining the dualistic division between subjective and objective, inner and outer, private and public.

14. The extracted philosophy of mind

From Shanahan’s work, we can extract a comprehensive philosophy of mind in twelve theses.

First, anti-dualism. The basic error in much philosophy of mind is the picture of consciousness as a private, hidden, metaphysical item. This error appears not only in Cartesian substance dualism, but also in modern debates that assume a private phenomenal residue beyond all public manifestation.

Second, ordinary-language semantic discipline. The meaning of consciousness-language is rooted in embodied, social, public human practices. To understand “consciousness,” we examine use, not an occult referent.

Third, non-eliminativism. Shanahan does not deny experience. He denies that experience should be reified into a private object that explains the meaning of consciousness-language.

Fourth, scientific naturalism after metaphysical therapy. Once dualistic confusion is dissolved, empirical work can proceed. Neuroscience, psychology, ethology, robotics, and AI can investigate the mechanisms and capacities associated with consciousness.

Fifth, global access and integration. In cognitive-scientific terms, consciousness is associated with information becoming globally available to multiple specialist systems. This explains reportability, flexible control, cross-domain integration, imagination, planning, and relevance-sensitive cognition.

Sixth, embodied point of view. Consciousness is not merely global information processing. A conscious subject is an embodied, indexically organized point of view. The body anchors perception, action, affect, and the unity of the subject.

Seventh, sensorimotor imagination. Inner life is not a private theater. It is largely internalized sensorimotor activity: covert action, simulated perception, inner speech, anticipation, and affective rehearsal, some of which becomes globally available.

Eighth, dynamic realization. In brains, consciousness is not a static module but an unfolding dynamical process involving connectivity, metastable coalitions, and integration across distributed systems.

Ninth, deflationary selfhood. The self is not a Cartesian ego. A conscious subject can exist without a full autobiographical self, and the human self is itself a constructed, embodied, social, temporally extended pattern.

Tenth, possible-minds pluralism. Consciousness may take forms very unlike human consciousness. But as exoticism increases, our vocabulary may fragment. Awareness, emotion, agency, selfhood, moral standing, and intelligence may not travel together.

Eleventh, encounter-based artificial consciousness. Artificial consciousness is not settled by behavioral imitation alone, by internal mechanism alone, or by a scalar metric alone. Candidate systems must be encounterable: entities whose purposeful behavior and mechanisms can be engaged, studied, and incorporated into our shared conceptual life.

Twelfth, normative conceptual responsibility. How we speak about AI systems matters. Over-attributing consciousness may mislead, manipulate, or degrade human relations; under-attributing it could become morally dangerous if genuinely encounterable artificial beings emerge. The right response requires technological literacy, philosophical clarity, and moral caution.

15. Comparison with major positions

Against Cartesian dualism, Shanahan denies that consciousness is an inner private substance or realm. His whole project aims to dissolve the inner/outer split.

Against Chalmers-style hard-problem realism, Shanahan denies that there is a well-formed residual metaphysical problem after all physical, functional, behavioral, and linguistic facts are in place. He sees the hard problem as an artifact of dualistic grammar.

Against Nagelian unknowability, he resists the idea that there are inaccessible subjective facts beyond all possible human conceptual reach. His answer to exotic minds is not “there is a hidden fact we can never know,” but “we need to ask whether and how our language can get a grip through encounter.”

Against behaviorism, he insists on mechanisms, brains, bodies, and internal simulation. Public manifestation matters, but not because consciousness is mere behavior. Behavior, embodiment, and mechanism together form the field in which consciousness-language has application.

Against standard functionalism, he uses functional architecture but rejects metaphysical functionalism as a final theory of mind-body identity. Global workspace is an empirical model, not a metaphysical essence.

Against identity theory, he does not reduce consciousness to a specific neural state type. Neural dynamics matter, but because they realize the capacities and forms of integration associated with consciousness, not because they reveal a simple identity.

Against pure integrated-information-style metric approaches, he objects that consciousness-ascription cannot be detached from behavior, embodiment, purpose, and interaction. A high value of some internal measure is not enough to establish consciousness in the ordinary or morally significant sense.

Relative to Dennett, Shanahan is close in anti-Cartesian spirit, but more explicitly tied to global workspace architecture, embodiment, robotics, and the space of possible minds.

Relative to enactivism, he shares the emphasis on embodiment and world-involvement, but he remains more hospitable to computational modeling, internal simulation, and global broadcast.

Relative to mainstream global neuronal workspace theory, he is aligned scientifically, but more philosophically cautious: the workspace explains conscious access and integration, but does not license a new metaphysical essence of consciousness.

16. Strengths and unresolved tensions

The strongest feature of Shanahan’s philosophy is its refusal of false choices. It is anti-dualist without being anti-scientific. It is computational without being disembodied. It is embodied without rejecting internal architecture. It is skeptical about current AI consciousness without dismissing future artificial minds. It is philosophically therapeutic while still practically engaged.

A second strength is that it treats artificial consciousness as a real conceptual and moral problem rather than a parlor puzzle. Shanahan saw that AI forces philosophy of mind out of the armchair: if large numbers of people treat AI systems as conscious, if companies market them as companions, if users form attachments, and if moral standing is debated, then philosophy has practical work to do.

A third strength is his “encounter” model. It avoids both naïve Turing-test behaviorism and hidden-essence metaphysics. It asks for sustained interaction, purposeful behavior, mechanistic understanding, and conceptual integration.

The first major tension is between his anti-theoretical Wittgensteinian stance and his constructive cognitive science. On one page, he says philosophy should dissolve metaphysical questions; on another, he offers global workspace architecture and neural dynamics. The best reconciliation is to distinguish levels: Wittgenstein governs the metaphysical grammar of the problem, while cognitive science explains the capacities that consciousness-language tracks. But critics may suspect that the science smuggles in a theory of consciousness after all.

The second tension concerns embodiment. Shanahan often treats embodiment as foundational, but his own edge cases—virtual agents, mixed-reality companions, locked-in patients, uploaded minds, simulated worlds—make it difficult to say how much embodiment is required. His mature answer is deliberately non-stipulative: philosophy should not legislate answers in advance; society must work through the cases. That is subtle, but it may frustrate anyone seeking necessary and sufficient conditions.

The third tension is moral. If consciousness-ascription depends partly on public criteria and society-wide conversation, what protects us from collective error? Shanahan answers that the conversation must be informed, philosophically literate, technologically literate, and anchored in form of life rather than opinion. Still, the risk remains that social power, corporate incentives, or anthropomorphic design could distort consciousness-talk.

The fourth tension is the phenomenal realist objection. A philosopher committed to qualia realism will say Shanahan has changed the subject: even if consciousness-language is public, surely there remains something it is like to feel pain. Shanahan’s reply is that this objection itself is powered by the dualistic picture. Whether one finds that satisfying depends on whether one accepts the Wittgensteinian therapy.

The fifth tension concerns LLMs. Shanahan’s role-play model is powerful, but as agents acquire persistent memory, world models, tools, embodiment, and social continuity, the distinction between role-play and authenticity may erode. Shanahan acknowledges this: for beliefs, goals, and intentions, the gap can close; for consciousness, the matter is harder, but future embodied simulacra may force conceptual change.

17. Final synthesis

Shanahan’s philosophy of mind can be summarized as follows.

The mind is not an immaterial theater. Consciousness is not a hidden glow. The self is not a Cartesian owner of experience. Human consciousness is an embodied, socially intelligible, dynamically realized form of global access, action-oriented integration, and sensorimotor-affective inner life. The language we use for consciousness is anchored in shared human life, but it can be extended—carefully, controversially, and perhaps unpredictably—to animals, aliens, robots, virtual agents, and other possible minds.

Artificial consciousness, on this view, is not a matter of finding the secret spark inside a machine. It is a matter of whether a system becomes the kind of entity with which we can share a world, discern purpose, study mechanisms, sustain interaction, attribute awareness, and responsibly reshape our concepts. Disembodied LLMs are especially difficult because they speak in human-like ways while lacking the bodily, temporal, worldly, and self-integrating structures that normally ground consciousness-language. Future embodied or virtually embodied AI may be different, but the question will demand philosophical literacy, empirical seriousness, and moral restraint.

Historically, Shanahan stands at a pivotal point in philosophy of mind. He inherits the anti-Cartesian work of Wittgenstein and Dennett, the architectural ambitions of functionalism and global workspace theory, the embodied turn in cognitive science, and the AI tradition’s concern with relevance, agency, and possible minds. His distinctive contribution is to bring these together just as artificial agents are becoming socially present. In that sense, his work marks a transition from twentieth-century debates about the metaphysics of consciousness to a twenty-first-century philosophy of mind in which the central question is not only “What is consciousness?” but “How should we think, speak, build, encounter, and morally respond as new forms of mind-like entity enter our world?”

PreviousRon Chrisley NextJames Reggia

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
